item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got this through the random button, and I'm debating whether this or the paper mache king is the most ridiculous SCP I've ever beheld. You referenced the killdozer and made an inside joke about trumpets, so you gain my +.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I have found the words that eluded me previously.
I do not know what the best way for me to restate my thoughts, but editing the original seems bad because the comments in reply would stop making sense, so just going to reply.
A pokemon reference brings nothing of value to the site. Does it really make a difference, or add anything, to establish that the Pokemon brand exists in the SCP universe? There is wasted potential with this, how many anomalous video game companies are there on the wiki, and more importantly why does it seem that so few of them are fleshed out?
I understand that authors are entitled to their visions, but I cannot shake the feeling that this could be better and add more depth to the site.
What I cannot stand about this is that it drops a reference to a real world video game but doesn't bring anything new to the table.
To contrast, the SCP that is 3 guys with Xbox 360s built into them fleshes out the CoTBG in a neat and believable way. Many real world churches find it hard to get their message to reach youth, and having met some rather strange christian gamers online, that one feels very much real way. It makes the CoTBG seem more believable.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Nah. I don't think it really adds anything to the article.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: proposal vetoed by author

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

It's stated that if Mega Charizard uses Dragon Dance, SCP-2466-2 preforms some weird, creepy synchronized dance. You know what else causes synchronized dance? SCP-2458.

Editing this:

SCP-2466-2 will converge in a public space and perform synchronized dances while repeatedly singing the song Pokémon Theme from the English adaptation of the Pokémon anime.

To this:

SCP-2466-2 will converge in a public space and perform synchronized dances1 while repeatedly singing the song Pokémon Theme from the English adaptation of the Pokémon anime.

Footnotes
1. Similarities have been noted between this synchronized dancing and the movements caused by SCP-2458.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gah, I find the image of someone scratching with bleeding boney hands to be heavily disturbing.

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the Wikipedia article for barbecue sauce.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fail to see the relevance of this link

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This really made me thonk with my head noggo.

EDIT: Oh my fucking god, shut the fuck up 2018 me. This SCP still slaps btw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barbecue_sauce

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Pop culture and video game references don't work when done straight. Just as with main series SCPs about mythology or folklore, it generally won't work unless you add a healthy dose of Foundation-flavored twist to it. Evangelions and portal guns simply won't cut it."
This is too straight for my tastes. I mean, a Halloween event? A hoenn trumpet joke?
Lets be real, this is good, but it has no place on the main list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One member of SCP-2466-2 played a trumpet rather than sing. The event concluded with SCP-2466-2 attempting to flood the church at which they had gathered.

Coincides with the North American release of the games Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire.

Hoenn. Trumpets. On the SCP Wiki.

( ͡° ͜ʖ ͡°)

+1'd so, so hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …I never thought I'd see a Killdozer reference on here. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I explained why that line was included in-universe.

You're trying to establish a thematic explanation, but it clashes: like I said, it's impossible to actually win or lose and thus no opportunity to do either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I explained why that line was included in-universe. If you want to disregard that and think of it as gameplay advice, you can do that. But when you don't like the explanation that you came up with, that's not my problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Listen, if the game would just roll Char's moves randomly, it would still probably be difficult to win, because it'd quickly go to +6 while you keep rolling Wurmples, your best bet is hoping for a Sturdy rock-type with a rock attack or something like that.

I'm asking "how does it matter" because there's no winning. If the Char faints, it just gets replaced by another char, the different people are rolled for every move every time, there's zero need for advice on how to play the game because there is no game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll round my 7.8/10 to a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll do it for twenty bucks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about The Bittercold from Pokemon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely nothing beyond the first paragraph of the description is relevant if the containment procedures are followed. Sometimes I'll ponder that question when writing an article, but more often than not it just gets in the way of storytelling.

I decided to include an overview of Pokemon battling so that readers, both in-universe and out-of-universe, can get an idea of what actually happens when you play 2466-1, whether you're familiar with Pokemon or not. I maybe could have gotten away with not doing so, but it could alienate a chunk of the readership.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…Can you imagine some level 0 researcher assigned to this?

"Jenkins, this new object is a Charimazard from that Pokeymans my grandkids like! What color is it?"

"I'll check Bulbapedia right away, sir!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like you could drop all the stuff explaining the basic mechanics of Pokemon. Since procedure is to disconnect the power source if the game is started, and not to play the game, describing them seems irrelevant. You've already said that the game uses Pokemon's battle mechanics by that point, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah. That would entail leaving it uncontained, in-universe, far longer than I find plausible.

The only reason I did a Hoenn joke was because there were memes about it that I could reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Will this be updated to include a Sun and Moon reference?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I never thought a serious pokemon SCP could be done with this much attention to real pokemon gameplay mechanics and still be great article.

+1, especially liked the Team Aqua reference

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"…after which SCP-2466-2 will set fire to the nearest building and disperse."

I had a very good laugh from how quickly that escalated. In my own opinion, if I had written it, I would have labeled it as a Joke SCP, but people are liking it just the way it is so nicely done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. You learn something new every day, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it feels like there's a huge chunk missing from the SCProcedures (or recovery?) detailing Foundation intervention. It's a little SOD shattering to imagine a few dozen people spontaneously immolating themselves without the Foundation doing anything about it/remarking on cleanup operations.

That's a story for another time, both in- and out-of-universe. Was it a big deal? Yeah, but I saw no pressing reason to distract from the article with a throwaway reference to that time the Foundation instated martial law on a mid-sized California city.

If this thing is meant to facilitate esoteric combat against a mighty dragon, why do the affected people use the dragon's moves? Wouldn't it make more sense for them to use the PC's moves?

Maybe I should amend my explanation: someone wants to save their town from a dragon, but none is forthcoming, so they copy one from Pokémon and make an app that lets them fight it and slay it, over and over and over. They don't give this app to anyone else — they don't want to share the glory. Letting anyone act out the user's moves would be sharing the glory.

The dragon is a game artifact. 2466-2 acts out ZardX's actions to give the game actual stakes.

Your idea is one direction I could have gone with this, but it would have a very different feel to it by necessity, and I would consider a different article entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The game presents a desperate situation, but randomly gives the user opportunities to achieve victory. Potentially speaks to the motivations of the game's creators.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In retrospect, it might be interesting if each Megazard-X spawned corresponds to a conventional combination of ideal moves for the Pokemon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The day when SCP Foundation site members are talking about the Pokemon metagame on an SCP discussion thread… God!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Due to Mega Charizard X's high power, few Pokémon assigned to the user can avoid fainting from both Flare Blitz and Dragon Claw after it has used Dragon Dance. However, the AI that determines Mega Charizard X's moves frequently makes strategic errors, which gives the user frequent opportunities to launch counterattacks.

How does it matter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As much as I'd love to upvote the hell out of this, there's one major issue keeping me from doing so.

Based off of how many people this thing controls, and how much it had apparently been used (as well as the severity/visibility of their actions) it feels like there's a huge chunk missing from the SCProcedures (or recovery?) detailing Foundation intervention. It's a little SOD shattering to imagine a few dozen people spontaneously immolating themselves without the Foundation doing anything about it/remarking on cleanup operations.

Someone wanted to save their town from a dragon

This. I feel this could be a helluva lot more awesome if this dragon had more of a presence in the article. Right now, I'm not seeing it. If this thing is meant to facilitate esoteric combat against a mighty dragon, why do the affected people use the dragon's moves? Wouldn't it make more sense for them to use the PC's moves?

It seems like the dragon is the only one getting any mileage out of the device. If it didn't exist, what the hell is the dragon doing that it needs to be slain in the first place? If the dragon existed in some form outside of the game, and somehow negatively affected the town, the Foundation would have to keep playing the game to defeat it.

So, if the # of affected civilians is reduced (let's say to one), given more opportunities to 'fight' the dragon (by using the randomly chosen player's pokemon's moveset), and the dragon exists beyond the game (and is, preferably, an asshole) then you have an article where the Foundation has no choice but to keep fighting an eldritch horror with the power of love and friendship pokemon!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think my response to your question at first didn't really clarify things because the use of present tense didn't click for me.

Nobody is using it currently. It was used before containment by some other party. Then they contained it, and used it a bit to identify its properties. Then they stopped using it and implemented the current containment procedures, and -2 stopped happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My concern is that if the scip is being safeguarded at the storage, then who is interacting with the game?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'Random' implies that the Foundation has not identified any pattern or tendencies in SCP-2466-2 selection. To say whether anyone was selected twice is basically the birthday problem. If this online calculator is reliable, then for a suburb of 30,000 people there would only have to be about 200 subjects before people are likely to show up twice. Ridiculous, right?

The tipping point changes depending on how big you imagine the city is… it seems reasonable that 50 activations of 11 on average happened, right? Only in a city of ~220,000 people could that happen and probably not have any repeats. There are sixteen cities like this in California, a few of which have nine-character names.

So basically, there are repeats; the city is San Diego, Santa Ana, or Riverside; or both.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. There are a lot of ways to weaken or get rid of Heatran, and just in terms of raw damage against neutral targets, Dragon Claw does a lot more than Earthquake. It also turns opposing Dragon-types from good checks into KOs. You could run it over Roost, but it'd be a shame not to run Roost on something with good bulk and a fantastic defensive typing (and a SR weakness), so Zard-X usually foregoes EQ. Unless it's the bulky WoW set, which is supposed to cripple traditional Zard X checks, but this ain't that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And this is yet another reason why you should not play Pokémon GO kids!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me a lot of SCP 2188 but on a smaller scale. I also have a few questions - Has it affected the same individuals more than once? If Mega Charizard X uses different moves over a short span of time, does it select a new group of individuals per move?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one. +1.

…Though, do people really use both D-Dance and Roost on Charizard X? That seems a bit weird to me. I know it's the recommended OU moveset, but does it actually work?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dragon Dance: SCP-2466-2 will converge in a public space and perform synchronized dances while repeatedly singing the song Pokémon Theme from the English adaptation of the Pokémon anime. This lasts for approximately fifteen minutes, after which SCP-2466-2 will set fire to the nearest building and disperse.

… I was thinking about how this could apply to so many of my friends, and then suddenly realized the last sentence existed. Could probably still apply to them though.

+1 with all the force up a thousand sun-stones and sunflora

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… yeah, I went in there with a color picker and despite my expectations, Mega Charizard X does seem to be a pretty standard gray. I guess its juxtaposition with blue made it look brown in comparison.

I'm going to leave it as black in the article, though, because that's what the relevant official website calls it, and I could easily see the Foundation going straight to the source to get the official word on its color.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dark grey is confirmed. Might need to check your screen settings, there. Or maybe ask someone if you look yellow, because jaundice can tint your vision like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God fucking dammit. Someone get a scarf Lando-T or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh! That's a different scenario!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't you kind of already do that one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am completely surprised that anyone dares to pull it off. I wanted to try a Pokemon skip once, but I couldn't imagine the ending point… Guess anything is possible nowadays. Hats (and heads) off to Cww.

Based on a dream I had

Time to write an SCP based on that dream where my consciousness is inside both a car and a velociraptor…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Either I've done something wrong with my computer settings, or that is not a brown dragon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. Looks brown to me. Bulbapedia says it's black… and so does pokemonxy.com. I'll edit it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What in the hell. It's so deeply rooted in pop culture that it flips around the dial to "is a good idea and can be taken seriously".

Actually, it reminds me of that skip where the one dude's life affects people in a town in Maine or somesuch. My only real question is, I thought Mega Charizard X was grey and blue, not brown. <.<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scantron my sides are in low earth orbit. I expect you and Elon Musk to launch a mission to recover them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If -2's actions are due to the moves in the game, does it mean that someone is interacting with the game, and won't it cause property damage?

Yeah. I assumed that was implicit — does something in the article contradict this? I hadn't changed the title yet because I'm in class and didn't know for sure that it would survive, but I'll change the series list later tonight. Or right now, whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok I'm curious about something.
If -2's actions are due to the moves in the game, does it mean that someone is interacting with the game, and won't it cause property damage?

Other than that, its an interesting read. +1

E: the title hasn't been change yet I think.

item.title: new
item.content:encoded:
Based on a dream I had, modified with input from a few friends. As far as I know, this is the first article on the wiki to reference Pokemon extensively, so if you need some context, check out this article: http://bulbapedia.bulbagarden.net/wiki/Charizard. However, I did try to write this so that the reader doesn't need any particular knowledge of Pokemon for it to work.

The premise, if you care, is: Someone wanted to save their town from a dragon, so they made an app on their phone to do it. But they were lazy, so instead of making their own dragon or mechanics, they just grabbed some from Pokemon.
